concept: Lie Down / Recline / Lower the Body to Rest
description: To lie down or recline: the body lowered into horizontal rest. Completes Phi's set of the four postures — 'whano' (stand), 'thalo' (walk), 'meilo' (sit), 'ruemi' (lie down) — the traditional postures in which mindfulness is sustained. Distinct from 'nulae' (sleep, the state) and 'therilu' (rest, the pause): 'ruemi' is the posture itself.
sound_symbolism: The liquid 'ru' begins the easing downward, the open hiatus 'u.e' is the slow fall of the body giving its weight away, and the inward 'mi' arrives at stillness — the self, settled flat. Spoken with full hiatus, the word takes exactly as long as lying down should.
grammatical_notes: Intransitive: 'mia ruemi' (I lie down). With place: 'mia nia nuwera ruemi' (I lie down on the bed). The Metta Sutta's ninth verse used 'therilu' (rest) for this posture before the word existed; both readings now stand.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: The fourth posture of practice: mindfulness sustained even lying down. The sutta's instruction needed this word, and now has it.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Rest honored as bodily wisdom — lying down at midday, with the sun, without shame.
